section.title: The Mind of Christ
section.sectionRationale: Here the letter turns to its central exhortation and its theological summit. Paul calls the church to a life worthy of the gospel, standing firm and united even under opposition, then grounds unity in humility that counts others more important than self. To fuel that humility he sets before them the mind of Christ, sung in the great hymn of the Son who emptied Himself to the point of death on a cross and was exalted by God to the name above every name.
section.thesis: Believers are to live worthy of the gospel, standing firm and united without fear of opponents, pursuing a humility that puts others first, and this humility is fueled by the mind of Christ, who though in the form of God emptied Himself, took the form of a servant, and became obedient to death on a cross, for which God exalted Him and gave Him the name above every name, that every knee should bow and every tongue confess Him Lord.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the call to a gospel-worthy, united, fearless life; the appeal to humility that considers others more important; and the hymn of Christ's self-emptying descent and God's exaltation of Him to the name above every name.

verses.text: Nevertheless, {{politeuomai|conduct yourselves as citizens}} in a manner worthy of the gospel of Christ. Then, whether I come and see you or only hear about you in my absence, I will know that you stand firm in one spirit, contending together as one for the faith of the gospel,
without being frightened in any way by those who oppose you. This is a clear sign of their destruction but of your salvation, and it is from God.
For it has been granted to you on behalf of Christ not only to believe in Him, but also to suffer for Him,
since you are encountering the same struggle you saw I had, and now hear that I still have.
Therefore if you have any encouragement in Christ, if any comfort from His love, if any fellowship with the Spirit, if any affection and compassion,
then make my joy complete by being like-minded, having the same love, being united in spirit and purpose.
Do nothing out of selfish ambition or empty pride, but in {{tapeinophrosyne|humility}} consider others more important than yourselves.
Each of you should look not only to your own interests, but also to the interests of others.
Let this mind be in you which was also in Christ Jesus:

units.label: The mind of Christ: descent and exaltation
units.note: he emptied himself, God exalted him

verses.text: Who, existing in the {{morphe|form}} of God, did not consider equality with God {{harpagmos|something to be grasped}},
but {{kenoo|emptied Himself}}, taking the form of a servant, being made in human likeness.
And being found in appearance as a man, He humbled Himself and became obedient to death, even death on a cross.
Therefore God {{hyperypsoo|exalted Him to the highest place}} and gave Him the name above all names,
that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, in heaven and on earth and under the earth,
and every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is {{kyrios|Lord}}, to the glory of God the Father.

politeuomai.gloss: To live as a citizen, to conduct one's public life. The word would ring in a Roman colony proud of its citizenship. Paul tells the Philippians their true citizenship is in the gospel, so they must live as worthy citizens of Christ's kingdom, a theme he returns to in 3:20.

tapeinophrosyne.gloss: Humility, lowliness of mind. In the ancient world this was often a term of contempt, the mindset of a slave; the gospel transforms it into a virtue. It is not thinking poorly of oneself but freely counting others more important, the very mindset displayed by Christ in the verses that follow.

morphe.gloss: Form, the outward expression of an inward and essential nature. To exist in the form of God is to possess the very nature and status of God, not a mere appearance. The same word describes His taking the form of a servant, a real assumption of the servant's nature, not a disguise.

harpagmos.gloss: Something to be seized, grasped, or clung to for advantage. Though equal with God, the Son did not treat that equality as a prize to exploit for Himself; instead He chose the path of self-giving. The word frames the whole hymn as the opposite of grasping ambition.

kenoo.gloss: To empty, to make of no reputation. The Son emptied Himself not by ceasing to be God but by taking the form of a servant, adding humanity and veiling His glory in obedience. This is the word behind the debated term kenosis; Scripture presents it as self-giving addition and lowliness, not subtraction of deity.

hyperypsoo.gloss: To exalt to the highest place, to raise supremely high. In answer to the Son's self-humbling to the depth of the cross, God raised Him to the utmost height and gave Him the name above every name. The word marks the great reversal at the hymn's turning point, the descent answered by exaltation.

kyrios.gloss: Lord, master, the word the Greek Old Testament used for the divine name. To confess Jesus Christ as Lord is to give Him the title of God Himself. The universal confession fulfills Isaiah 45, where every knee bows and every tongue swears to the LORD, now directed to Jesus, to the glory of the Father.

basic.context: Paul now gives the church its marching orders: live as citizens worthy of the gospel, stand together, and do not be scared by those who oppose you, for suffering for Christ is itself a gift. Unity like that grows only from humility, so Paul tells them to count others more important than themselves. Then he lifts their eyes to the supreme example, Christ, who though He was God did not cling to His rights but emptied Himself, became a servant, and obeyed all the way to a criminal's cross, so God raised Him high and gave Him the name above every name.
basic.hermeneutics: The exhortation and the hymn belong together. Paul is not merely teaching doctrine about Christ; he is holding up Christ's mind as the pattern for the church's life. The hymn of verses 6 to 11 is the theological engine that drives the practical call to humility and unity.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul tells them to politeuomai, to live as citizens, worthy of the gospel. Unity grows from tapeinophrosyne, humility. The hymn says the Son was in the morphe, the form and nature, of God, yet did not treat equality with God as harpagmos, a thing to grasp; instead He did kenoo, emptied Himself, taking a servant's form. So God did hyperypsoo, supremely exalted Him, that every tongue confess Jesus as kyrios, Lord.
basic.typology: Christ is the true and obedient man where Adam grasped to be like God, the Son let go and obeyed. He is the servant of Isaiah who is exalted after suffering. And the universal confession fulfills Isaiah 45, where God swears that every knee will bow to Him alone, now fulfilled in Jesus.
basic.christ: This hymn is one of the highest views of Christ in Scripture. He is God by nature, yet He humbled Himself to the lowest point, death on a cross, and God exalted Him to the highest place, giving Him the divine name and universal worship. The path down into servanthood and up to the throne is the very shape of the gospel.
basic.god: God the Father is the one who exalts the Son and to whose glory every confession is made. We see a God whose greatness is displayed in self-giving love, who honors the humble, and who is glorified precisely in the exaltation of the crucified Christ as Lord of all.
basic.crossReferences: John 13 shows Jesus washing feet, the mind of the servant. Isaiah 45 promises every knee will bow to God. 2 Corinthians 8:9 says the rich Christ became poor for our sake.
basic.oneStory: Where Adam reached up to seize equality with God and fell, the Son who truly was equal with God stooped down, obeyed, and lifted humanity with Him. The whole story of grasping and falling is reversed by the One who let go and was exalted, and it ends with all creation confessing Him Lord.

context.literary: Philippians 1:27 begins the letter's central exhortation, and 2:1 to 4 presses it toward humility. Verses 5 to 11 form a self contained hymn, arranged as a descent, verses 6 to 8, and an answering exaltation, verses 9 to 11, hinged on the Therefore of verse 9. Its placement makes it not an abstract creed but the model for the humility just commanded.
context.historical: Philippi's pride in Roman citizenship gives Paul's call to live as worthy citizens of the gospel its edge. The church faced external opposition and internal friction, hints of which surface later in the plea to Euodia and Syntyche. Into this Paul sets a hymn many scholars think was already sung in the early church.
context.canonical: The hymn stands with John 1, Colossians 1, and Hebrews 1 as a summit of New Testament Christology, uniting the Son's full deity, real incarnation, atoning death, and cosmic exaltation. Its climax deliberately applies Isaiah 45:23, God's exclusive claim to universal worship, to the Lord Jesus.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To call the Philippians to a united, courageous, gospel-worthy life rooted in humility, and to ground that humility in the example and glory of Christ, whose self-emptying obedience and exaltation are both the pattern for their life and the assurance that the humble path ends in glory.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Verses 27 to 2:5 are direct commands: live worthy, stand firm, be humble, have this mind. Verses 6 to 11 are declaration, the settled truth of who Christ is and what He did, offered as the ground and motive for the commands. The hymn is not prescribing that we save others by dying but that we adopt Christ's self-giving mindset.

debates.question: What does it mean that Christ emptied Himself (kenosis)?

views.label: Self-giving addition, not loss of deity
views.case: He emptied Himself by taking the form of a servant and being made in human likeness; the emptying is His humbling and adding of humanity, veiling His glory in obedience, not surrendering His divine nature.

views.label: Laying aside divine attributes
views.case: Some kenotic theories argue the Son gave up certain divine attributes in the incarnation; this strains the text and endangers His full deity, which the hymn everywhere assumes.

debates.bearing: The orthodox reading keeps Christ fully God even in the manger and on the cross; the emptying is the greatness of His condescension, not a diminishing of His deity.

debates.question: Does suffering for Christ being granted mean God authors the church's persecution?

views.label: A gracious gift and privilege
views.case: Paul says it has been granted, freely given, to suffer for Christ, treating persecution endured for the gospel as a grace that unites believers to Christ and confirms their salvation.

views.label: A grim fate to be merely endured
views.case: Reading it only as fate misses Paul's point; he frames suffering for Christ as a privilege from God, a sign of belonging to Christ, not a bare misfortune.

debates.bearing: The verse dignifies Christian suffering as a God-given fellowship with Christ, steadying believers so opposition does not frighten them.

study.originalLanguages: Paul opens with politeuomai, to live as a citizen, a pointed word in a proud Roman colony: their true commonwealth is the gospel. The bridge to the hymn is tapeinophrosyne, humility or lowliness of mind, once a term of contempt, now the crowning virtue. The hymn's key words map its movement. The Son exists in the morphe of God, the very form and nature of deity, yet did not regard equality with God as harpagmos, a prize to be seized and exploited. Instead He performed kenoo, He emptied Himself, defined at once as taking the form of a servant, self-giving, not self-stripping. In answer God did hyperypsoo, super-exalted Him, and the goal is that every tongue confess Jesus as kyrios, Lord, the Greek Old Testament's word for the divine name, fulfilling Isaiah 45:23.

typology.fulfillment: Where Adam grasped to be like God and fell, Christ, truly equal with God, did not grasp but humbled Himself and obeyed, undoing Adam's fall.
Christ fulfills Isaiah's servant who is humbled unto death and then lifted high and honored by God.
The universal worship fulfills Isaiah 45:23, God's exclusive claim to every knee and tongue, now given to the Lord Jesus to the Father's glory.

study.christ: The hymn presents Christ in the full arc of the gospel. Existing in the form of God, sharing the divine nature, He refused to cling to His rights and instead emptied Himself, taking a servant's form, becoming truly human, and obeying all the way to the shameful death of the cross. Because of this the Father exalted Him supremely, granting Him the name above every name, the divine name, so that all creation, in heaven, on earth, and under the earth, will bow and confess Him Lord. He is fully God, truly man, the crucified and risen King before whom every knee will bend.
study.god: God the Father stands at both ends of the hymn: the equality the Son would not exploit is equality with God, and the exaltation of the Son is God's own act, to God's own glory. The passage reveals a God whose glory shines in humble, self-giving love, who lifts up the lowly, and who is most glorified when the crucified Christ is confessed as Lord by all.

crossReferences.note: Every knee shall bow and every tongue swear to the LORD, applied to Jesus in verses 10 to 11.
Jesus, knowing all authority was His, washes feet, the mind of the servant enacted.
Though rich, He became poor for our sake, the same downward movement of grace.
A parallel hymn confessing the Son's deity, humbling, and supremacy over all things.

study.oneStory: The Bible's story pivots on two reachings. Adam reached up to seize equality with God and plunged humanity into ruin. The Son, who truly possessed equality with God, reached down, emptied Himself, obeyed unto death, and was exalted as Lord. In Him the fall is reversed and the whole creation is drawn toward the day when every knee bows and every tongue confesses Jesus Christ as Lord, to the glory of God the Father.

quiz.q: What does Paul mean by conducting yourselves in a manner worthy of the gospel?

options.text: Living as citizens of Christ's kingdom, standing firm and united for the faith.
options.note: Verse 27 uses citizenship language, calling for a united, gospel-shaped public life.

options.text: Earning the gospel by impressive moral effort.
options.note: The gospel is received by faith; worthy conduct is its fruit, not its price.

options.text: Keeping quiet so opponents leave you alone.
options.note: Paul calls them to contend for the faith without fear, not to hide.

options.text: Living however feels right to each person.
options.note: He calls for a life shaped by and worthy of the gospel of Christ.

quiz.q: According to verses 3 and 4, what is the root of Christian unity?

options.text: Humility that counts others more important and looks to their interests.
options.note: Verses 3 to 4 ground unity in humility, not selfish ambition.

options.text: Everyone insisting on their own rights.
options.note: That is selfish ambition, the opposite of what Paul commands.

options.text: Ignoring differences and never speaking honestly.
options.note: Unity comes from humble love, not from mere avoidance.

options.text: Competition to prove who is most important.
options.note: Paul forbids empty pride and selfish ambition.

quiz.q: What does it mean that Christ emptied Himself?

options.text: He took the form of a servant and became human, veiling His glory in obedience.
options.note: Verse 7 defines the emptying as taking a servant's form, not losing deity.

options.text: He gave up being God during His time on earth.
options.note: A common kenotic error; the hymn assumes His full deity throughout.

options.text: He only appeared to be human but was not.
options.note: He was made in human likeness and found in appearance as a man, truly human.

options.text: He stopped existing for a time.
options.note: The Son never ceased to be; He humbled Himself by adding humanity.

quiz.q: Why did God exalt Jesus to the highest place?

options.text: Because He humbled Himself and became obedient to death on a cross.
options.note: The Therefore of verse 9 links exaltation to His self-humbling obedience.

options.text: Because Jesus demanded to be honored.
options.note: He did not grasp; the exaltation is God's answer to His humility.

options.text: Because the crowds forced God's hand.
options.note: The exaltation is God's own free act in response to Christ's obedience.

options.text: For no particular reason.
options.note: Paul gives a clear reason, His obedience unto death.

quiz.q: What is the final goal of every knee bowing and tongue confessing Jesus as Lord?

options.text: The glory of God the Father.
options.note: Verse 11 ends with to the glory of God the Father.

options.text: The exaltation of the church.
options.note: The church shares in Christ's glory, but the stated goal is the Father's glory.

options.text: The pride of individual believers.
options.note: The hymn is about Christ's honor and the Father's glory, not human pride.

options.text: Nothing beyond the confession itself.
options.note: Paul names the goal, the glory of God the Father.

questions: Paul says even suffering for Christ is a gift granted to you. How does calling suffering a gift, rather than only a hardship, change the way you face opposition?
Humility means counting others more important than yourself. Where is God asking you to look to someone else's interests this week rather than your own?
Christ did not cling to His rights but let them go for our sake. What right or advantage are you tempted to grasp that Christ might be calling you to release?
The path of Christ went down into servanthood before God lifted Him up. Where are you seeking to rise that God may be calling you first to stoop?
One day every knee will bow to Jesus as Lord. How does living now in light of that certain future reshape your priorities today?

translationNotes.note: Something to be grasped renders harpagmos; the BSB footnote notes the alternative something to be exploited. Either way the Son did not use equality with God for His own advantage.
Emptied Himself translates ekenosen, the source of the term kenosis; the following words define it as taking a servant's form, not surrendering deity.
The BSB reads and became obedient to death, even death on a cross, with an em dash before even; here it is rendered with a comma to keep the flow, with no change to wording.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Philippians 1:27-2:11 this session. The em dash in verse 2:8 before even death on a cross is rendered as a comma to comply with the no-dash style rule; wording is unchanged.
